progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and relevant problem—learning identity-preserving transformations without explicit transformation labels—and the framing around Lie-group-based manifold models is generally clear and well motivated. The introduction does a good job identifying two limitations of prior work: dependence on labeled or hand-selected transformation pairs and the lack of locality/context in applying learned operators. However, the paper’s central claims would need sharper positioning and stronger justification. In particular, the proposed method is described as “unsupervised,” yet it relies on a pretrained classifier to assess identity preservation, which raises questions about supervision, classifier bias, and whether the learned transformations reflect the data manifold or the classifier’s invariances. The novelty relative to the Manifold Autoencoder and related Lie-operator approaches is also somewhat difficult to assess from the opening: it is clear that point-pair selection and local operator applicability are modified, but the technical contribution and its generality need to be articulated more precisely. The motivation is compelling, but several assumptions remain underexamined, especially that perceptual similarity can be recovered reliably without labels, that class identity corresponds to manifold identity, and that classifier confidence is an adequate proxy for semantic preservation. Clarity is mostly strong, though there are minor writing issues and some overbroad claims about “natural transformations” and manifold structure that should be tempered or empirically substantiated. Overall, the paper appears promising and potentially useful, especially if experiments convincingly show semantically meaningful transformations beyond simple datasets, but its main risks are conceptual: dependence on supervised components, possible circularity in defining identity preservation, and the need for clearer evidence that the learned operators capture genuine manifold transformations rather than artifacts of the training heuristic or classifier.

comments.title: Prior Lie-group approaches are described too broadly as requiring transformation labels
comments.quote: However theses approaches are limited because they require transformation labels when training their models and they lack a method for determining which regions of the manifold are appropriate for applying each specific operator.
comments.explanation: The abstract's wording can be read as claiming that all prior learned Lie-group/operator approaches require transformation labels. Later in the paper, the authors give a more nuanced taxonomy: some methods use predefined groups, some require transformation labels for selecting point pairs, and some rely on weaker class-based or random pair selection. The likely intended point is that existing approaches either assume known transformation structure, use transformation labels, or lack a way to learn local operator applicability. As written, however, the claim overstates the limitation of the whole literature and could mislead readers about the baselines.

comments.title: “Unsupervised” claim is ambiguous because the method relies on pretrained supervised classifiers
comments.quote: Additionally, we train on CelebA to showcase our model’s ability to learn semantically meaningful transformations on complex datasets in an unsupervised manner.
comments.explanation: The method appears not to require explicit transformation labels, and perhaps not labels on the target dataset, which is a meaningful distinction. However, the paper also assumes access to a pretrained classifier and uses its feature space or outputs to guide point-pair selection and identity-preserving coefficient statistics. If that classifier was trained with semantic labels, then the procedure is not unsupervised in the strict sense; it is better described as using external supervised features while avoiding target transformation labels. This is reinforced by the later description of classifier features as a supervision strategy. The paper should clarify whether “unsupervised” means no target-dataset labels, no transformation labels, or no supervision at all.

comments.title: Lie groups, Lie algebras, and the data manifold are conflated
comments.quote: A Lie group is a group of continuous transformations which also defines a manifold by representing infinitesimal transformations that can be applied to input data (Hoffman, 1966; Dodwell, 1983).
comments.explanation: The intended modeling picture is understandable: learned infinitesimal generators are exponentiated to produce finite transformations that move points along a learned manifold. But the exposition conflates several distinct objects. A Lie group is itself a smooth manifold with a group structure; infinitesimal transformations live in its Lie algebra; and the data manifold is a separate object on which the group acts. Similarly, a finite set of Lie algebra generators can define the orbit of a group action under suitable assumptions, but it does not generally globally define an arbitrary nonlinear data manifold, especially one with nontrivial topology or requiring multiple charts. The statement should be qualified as a modeling assumption for manifolds well approximated by group-generated transformations.

comments.title: Contractive autoencoder tangent estimation is oversimplified
comments.quote: The contractive autoencoder (CAE) estimates manifold tangents by minimizing the the Jacobian of the encoder network, encouraging invariance of latent vectors to image space perturbations (Rifai et al., 2011c;b;a; Kumar et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The paper points to the right family of results, but the mechanism is described too simply. A CAE minimizes a reconstruction loss plus a Frobenius-norm penalty on the encoder Jacobian; the reconstruction term prevents collapse, and the learned Jacobian is interpreted anisotropically. In the Rifai et al. view, dominant singular vectors of the Jacobian correspond to tangent directions, while contraction is encouraged primarily off the manifold. Saying that CAEs estimate tangents merely by minimizing the Jacobian could mislead readers about how tangent directions are recovered.

comments.title: Claim that pixel space cannot support identity-preserving smooth interpolation is too strong
comments.quote: For example, data in the pixel space suffers from the curse of dimensionality and cannot be smoothly interpolated while maintaining identity (Bengio et al., 2005).
comments.explanation: The intended motivation for learning latent manifolds is reasonable: naive Euclidean interpolation between arbitrary images often leaves the natural-image manifold and produces implausible blends. But “cannot” is too strong. Some identity-preserving changes, such as small translations, brightness changes, or appropriately constructed warps, can be represented as smooth paths in pixel space. The safer claim is that naive pixel-space interpolation is generally inefficient or perceptually poor, not that smooth identity-preserving interpolation is impossible.

comments.title: Coefficient-encoder objective is underspecified with respect to labels, classifier choice, and decoder composition
comments.quote: Given labeled observations ( **z** _, y_ ) and a pretrained classifier to produce augmented sampleson the latent space _r_ ( _·_ ), we aim to learn a network  **z** without changing the classifier output _qφ_ ( **c** _|_ **z** ) that outputs parameters that can be used _r_ ( **z** ) = _y_ .
comments.explanation: The coefficient encoder is central to the paper, but the training setup is ambiguous. The text appears to require labeled observations \((\mathbf z, y)\), which is not reconciled with earlier claims that class labels are unnecessary; perhaps the authors intend to use the pretrained classifier's prediction as a pseudo-label, but this is not clear. It is also not specified whether the classifier used for coefficient training is the same ImageNet ResNet used for nearest-neighbor pair selection, a task-specific classifier, or a classifier trained directly in latent space. Finally, because \(\mathbf z\) denotes an autoencoder latent vector, a pretrained image classifier would normally be applied after decoding, e.g. to \(g(\mathbf z)\) and \(g(T_\Psi(\mathbf c)\mathbf z)\), not directly to \(\mathbf z\). Without specifying the label source, classifier, and whether classification happens in input space or latent space, the objective is difficult to reproduce.

comments.title: KL-to-prior regularizer does not by itself imply “largest coefficient values possible”
comments.quote: Therefore, we want to encourage the largest coefficient values possible while maintaining the identity of our initial data point. This motivates the addition of a KL-divergence regularizer that encourages the distribution _qφ_ ( **c** _|_ **z** ) to be similar to a specified Laplace prior with a fixed scale _ζ_ like in (4).
comments.explanation: The motivation is sensible: a pure classifier-consistency loss can collapse toward zero transformations, so the model needs pressure toward nontrivial coefficients. But matching \(q_\phi(\mathbf c\mid\mathbf z)\) to a fixed-scale Laplace prior encourages coefficient scales near the chosen prior scale, not necessarily the largest identity-preserving values. The learned scale depends on the prior scale, KL weight, KL direction, classifier-consistency penalty, and operator normalization. Interpreting the resulting coefficients as maximal safe transformation magnitudes therefore requires additional justification.

comments.title: Classifier-based consistency is overinterpreted as identity, invariance, and boundary information
comments.quote: Second, we develop a method that uses a pretrained classifier (measuring identity-preservation of transformed samples) to learn the local regions where each operator is likely to be used while preserving the identity of transformed samples.
comments.explanation: Using classifier-output consistency is a practical proxy for preserving task-relevant semantics, but the paper sometimes states the interpretation too strongly. A classifier measures preservation of its own predicted label or output distribution, not identity preservation itself. Likewise, small learned coefficient scales indicate sensitivity of the pretrained classifier along learned operator directions, not necessarily closeness to a true semantic class boundary or a limit of identity-preserving transformations. Heatmaps of encoded scale weights can inform about classifier invariances, but those scales also depend on operator magnitudes, latent geometry, decoder behavior, classifier calibration, the KL prior, and training choices. The claims should consistently be qualified as statements about the pretrained classifier's decision function rather than the true data manifold or semantic identity.

comments.title: Use of ImageNet features for MNIST and Fashion-MNIST needs justification
comments.quote: We select training point pairs that are nearest neighbors in the feature space of the final, pre-logit layer of a ResNet-18 (He et al., 2015) classifier pretrained on ImageNet (Russakovsky et al., 2015).
comments.explanation: Using pretrained classifier features as a perceptual or semantic metric is reasonable for natural-image datasets such as CelebA. However, the paper earlier motivates this strategy by assuming a classifier pretrained on data with similar statistics to the target dataset. ImageNet statistics differ substantially from MNIST digits and Fashion-MNIST grayscale clothing images. The method may still work empirically, but the paper should clarify preprocessing and justify why ImageNet pre-logit features provide meaningful nearest neighbors for these datasets, or include an ablation using domain-specific or unsupervised features.

comments.title: Baseline interpolation, extrapolation, and CAE path construction are underspecified
comments.quote: For all other methods, Eucilidean distance between features is used for both interpolation and extrapolation.
comments.explanation: The comparison requires a reproducible path-generation procedure for each baseline, but the description is not sufficient. Euclidean distance between features is a metric, not an interpolation or extrapolation algorithm. The paper should specify the feature representation, path formula, extrapolation direction, step-size scaling, and how path lengths are matched to MAE paths. This is especially important for CAE baselines: a tangent plane contains many possible directions, and the text does not explain which tangent directions are selected, how they are normalized, how far they are followed, or whether tangents are recomputed along the path. These choices can materially affect the visual and quantitative comparisons.

comments.title: Identity-preservation evaluation does not define the reference class or establish true path accuracy
comments.quote: To quantify the identity preservation of each transformation, we input each generated image into a pretrained classifier and plot the prediction of the correct class.
comments.explanation: The evaluation uses a pretrained classifier to track whether generated paths preserve identity, but the protocol leaves key details unclear. For interpolations, the paper should state whether endpoint pairs are required to have the same class, whether the reference label is that of \(\mathbf x_0\), \(\mathbf x_1\), a ground-truth label, or the classifier's initial prediction. If nearest-neighbor pairs can have different labels, there is no single obvious “correct class” along the entire path. For extrapolation, the intended class is probably the starting label, but this is also not explicit. In addition, classifier accuracy measures preservation of a classifier prediction, not accuracy of an extrapolated manifold path, since no ground-truth extrapolated path is available. The claim should be framed as better classifier-label preservation rather than more accurate path estimation.

comments.title: Qualitative examples alone do not establish improved identity-preserving augmentation
comments.quote: While both strategies generate some realistic variations of the data, using the encoded scale weights improves identity-preservation of the transformed output. The augmentations with the encoded scale weights are better at maintaining the identity of the sampled points.
comments.explanation: The qualitative examples support the intuition that locally encoded coefficient scales may produce safer augmentations than a fixed global scale. However, the text states an improvement in identity preservation as a conclusion, while the passage appears to rely mainly on selected image examples. Because identity-preserving augmentation is a central benefit of the coefficient encoder, the comparison should ideally include quantitative evidence, such as label preservation, classifier-consistency rates on held-out samples, or human or attribute-based validation. The fixed-scale baseline should also be described well enough to judge whether the comparison is matched fairly.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and relevant problem—learning identity-preserving transformations without explicit transformation labels—and the framing around Lie-group-based manifold models is generally clear and well motivated. The introduction does a good job identifying two limitations of prior work: dependence on labeled or hand-selected transformation pairs and the lack of locality/context in applying learned operators. However, the paper’s central claims would need sharper positioning and stronger justification. In particular, the proposed method is described as “unsupervised,” yet it relies on a pretrained classifier to assess identity preservation, which raises questions about supervision, classifier bias, and whether the learned transformations reflect the data manifold or the classifier’s invariances. The novelty relative to the Manifold Autoencoder and related Lie-operator approaches is also somewhat difficult to assess from the opening: it is clear that point-pair selection and local operator applicability are modified, but the technical contribution and its generality need to be articulated more precisely. The motivation is compelling, but several assumptions remain underexamined, especially that perceptual similarity can be recovered reliably without labels, that class identity corresponds to manifold identity, and that classifier confidence is an adequate proxy for semantic preservation. Clarity is mostly strong, though there are minor writing issues and some overbroad claims about “natural transformations” and manifold structure that should be tempered or empirically substantiated. Overall, the paper appears promising and potentially useful, especially if experiments convincingly show semantically meaningful transformations beyond simple datasets, but its main risks are conceptual: dependence on supervised components, possible circularity in defining identity preservation, and the need for clearer evidence that the learned operators capture genuine manifold transformations rather than artifacts of the training heuristic or classifier.

comments.title: Prior Lie-group approaches are described as uniformly requiring transformation labels
comments.quote: However theses approaches are limited because they require transformation labels when training their models and they lack a method for determining which regions of the manifold are appropriate for applying each specific operator.
comments.explanation: At first, this reads as a broad claim about the whole class of learned Lie-group/operator approaches just mentioned. Looking later in the introduction, the authors give a more nuanced taxonomy: some prior work uses predefined groups such as SO(3), some requires transformation labels for selecting point pairs, and some randomly selects same-class pairs. That later context resolves the likely intended point—existing methods either rely on known transformation structure, transformation labels, or weaker class-based pairing, and do not learn local operator applicability. But the abstract’s wording is stronger than what the paper itself supports: not all of the cited approaches require transformation labels. This is mostly a framing/precision issue, but it could mislead readers about the baseline literature.

comments.title: “Unsupervised” claim is ambiguous given reliance on pretrained classifiers
comments.quote: Additionally, we train on CelebA to showcase our model’s ability to learn semantically meaningful transformations on complex datasets in an unsupervised manner.
comments.explanation: The intended meaning seems to be that the method does not require explicit transformation labels, and perhaps not labels on the target CelebA examples. That is a meaningful distinction, and the later methods section does say the point-pair selection avoids transformation labels. However, the full context also states that the method assumes access to a pretrained classifier, likely from a model zoo, and uses classifier features/output to guide both point-pair selection and identity-preserving coefficient statistics. If that classifier was trained with semantic labels, then the procedure is not unsupervised in the strict sense commonly used in machine learning; it is better described as using external supervision or pretrained supervised features while requiring no transformation labels on the target dataset. The current phrasing could lead readers to overestimate how label-free the approach is.

comments.title: Classifier consistency is treated as measuring identity preservation
comments.quote: Second, we develop a method that uses a pretrained classifier (measuring identity-preservation of transformed samples) to learn the local regions where each operator is likely to be used while preserving the identity of transformed samples.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that classifier-output consistency is a practical and standard proxy for preserving task-relevant identity, and later they more carefully say the method can describe the invariances of the classifier. Still, the parenthetical here says the classifier is “measuring identity-preservation,” which is stronger than what classifier predictions can guarantee. A classifier may be invariant to changes that humans would regard as identity-changing, may fail under distribution shift, or may use a coarser notion of identity than the data manifold concept requires. The concern is not that the method is invalid, but that the claim should be qualified as measuring preservation of the pretrained classifier’s predicted label or semantic output, not identity preservation itself.

comments.title: Global definition of a manifold by Lie operators is stated too generally
comments.quote:
Lie group operators represent infinitesimal transformations which can be applied to data

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

through an exponential mapping to transform points along a manifold, and a manifold can be globally defined by a set of operators that each move in different directions along it (Hoffman, 1966; Dodwell, 1983).
comments.explanation: The high-level intuition is appropriate for the model: infinitesimal generators can be exponentiated to produce transformations, and a learned dictionary of operators can parameterize directions of motion. However, as a mathematical statement this is too broad. A finite set of Lie algebra generators globally defines the orbit of a Lie group action under suitable assumptions; it does not generally globally define an arbitrary nonlinear data manifold, especially when the manifold has nontrivial topology, requires multiple charts, or is not homogeneous under a finite-dimensional group action. The rest of the paper appears to treat this as a modeling assumption and later adds local operator statistics, which partly mitigates the issue. Still, the sentence could mislead a careful reader into thinking the representation is generally valid for all manifolds rather than for manifolds well-approximated by such group-generated transformations.

comments.title: Conflation of Lie groups, Lie algebras, and the data manifold
comments.quote:
A Lie group is a group of continuous transformations which also defines a manifold by representing infinitesimal transformations that can be applied to input data (Hoffman,

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

1966; Dodwell, 1983). Several methods incorporate Lie groups into neural networks to represent data transformations that are identity-preserving within the model (Cohen & Welling, 2014; Cohen et al., 2018; Cosentino et al., 2021). A prevalent strategy is to learn a dictionary of Lie group _·_ operators that are mapped to a specific group element through the matrix exponential expm( )
comments.explanation: I understand the intended picture: the learned transport operators are infinitesimal generators, and exponentiating a linear combination of them gives a finite transformation that moves a point along the learned manifold. The following sentence about the matrix exponential partially resolves the intent. However, the wording still conflates three distinct objects under standard Lie theory: a Lie group is itself a smooth manifold with a group structure; infinitesimal transformations live in its Lie algebra/tangent space at the identity; and the data manifold is a separate object on which the transformations act. Saying that the Lie group “defines a manifold by representing infinitesimal transformations” is therefore technically imprecise. This matters because the learned matrices \(\Psi_m\) are best understood as Lie algebra generators/transport operators, not as group elements themselves, and the data manifold is not generally the same manifold as the Lie group.

comments.title: Contractive autoencoder tangent estimation is oversimplified
comments.quote: The contractive autoencoder (CAE) estimates manifold tangents by minimizing the the Jacobian of the encoder network, encouraging invariance of latent vectors to image space perturbations (Rifai et al., 2011c;b;a; Kumar et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The authors are pointing to the right family of results: CAEs use a penalty on the encoder Jacobian and have been used to recover local manifold structure. But the statement compresses an important technical point in a way that could be misleading. A CAE does not simply estimate tangent directions by minimizing the encoder Jacobian; it minimizes a reconstruction loss plus a Frobenius-norm Jacobian penalty. The reconstruction objective prevents collapse, and the learned Jacobian is then anisotropic: directions with larger sensitivity correspond to tangent directions, while contraction is encouraged primarily off the manifold. In the Rifai et al. interpretation, tangent directions are extracted from dominant singular vectors of the Jacobian, not from making the whole Jacobian small. The surrounding background context makes the broad intent clear, but the mechanism as written is not quite correct.

comments.title: Overstrong claim that pixel space cannot support identity-preserving smooth interpolation
comments.quote: For example, data in the pixel space suffers from the curse of dimensionality and cannot be smoothly interpolated while maintaining identity (Bengio et al., 2005).
comments.explanation: I read this as motivation for learning a latent manifold rather than doing Euclidean operations directly in image space, which is a standard and reasonable point. Still, the word “cannot” is too strong. Linear interpolation between arbitrary images in pixel space often leaves the natural-image manifold or produces perceptually implausible blends, and many identity-preserving transformations are highly nonlinear in raw pixels. But some identity-preserving changes can be represented as smooth pixel-space paths, for example small translations with interpolation, changes in brightness, or morphs constructed through appropriate warps. The more defensible claim is that naive Euclidean interpolation in pixel space is generally inefficient or perceptually poor, not that smooth identity-preserving interpolation is impossible in pixel space.

comments.title: “Unsupervised” point-pair selection relies on externally supervised classifier features
comments.quote: We generalize transport operator training to incorporate an unsupervised learning strategy that can be applied to a wide array of datasets. In particular, we assume access to a classifier pretrained on a large-scale dataset (likely taken from a model zoo) with similar statistics as our training dataset of interest (Nguyen et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: I understand the intended distinction: the method does not require transformation labels, and perhaps does not require labels from the specific target dataset. Using a pretrained classifier’s penultimate features as a perceptual metric is a reasonable and common transfer-learning strategy. However, calling the strategy simply “unsupervised” is potentially misleading because the semantic structure in those features usually comes from supervised pretraining on labeled data. The following sentence even describes these classifier features as a “supervision strategy.” If the claim is only that target-dataset transformation labels or target-dataset class labels are unnecessary, that should be stated explicitly. As written, a reader could reasonably infer a fully unsupervised method, whereas the method depends on external labeled supervision through the pretrained classifier.

comments.title: Use of target labels in coefficient-encoder training is not reconciled with the no-class-label framing
comments.quote: Given labeled observations ( **z** _, y_ ) and a pretrained classifier to produce augmented sampleson the latent space _r_ ( _·_ ), we aim to learn a network  **z** without changing the classifier output _qφ_ ( **c** _|_ **z** ) that outputs parameters that can be used _r_ ( **z** ) = _y_ .
comments.explanation: The surrounding section is trying to learn where each operator preserves identity, and using a classifier to test whether a transformed point keeps the same predicted class is a plausible approach. But this sentence appears to require labeled observations \((\mathbf z,y)\), which creates ambiguity relative to the earlier claim that the approach does not require class labels. It may be that the authors only need the pretrained classifier’s prediction as a pseudo-label, or that labels are needed only for this later local-statistics module and not for unsupervised operator learning. The current wording does not make that separation clear. This matters because the coefficient encoder is one of the paper’s main contributions; if it requires labels from the target dataset, the scope of the “without class labels” claim is narrower than the passage suggests.

comments.title: Classifier is applied to latent vectors without clearly specifying decoder composition
comments.quote: We specifically minimize the KL-divegence between the classifier output _r_ ( **z** ) and the estimated probability of the transformed output _r_ ( **z** ).
comments.explanation: The intended objective is understandable: sample coefficients from the local coefficient distribution, transform the latent code with the learned transport operators, and penalize changes in the classifier’s output. However, earlier notation defines the autoencoder latent vector \(\mathbf z\) separately from the data point \(\mathbf x\), while a pretrained classifier would normally operate on decoded images/data, not directly on MAE latent vectors. Thus the classifier should presumably be applied as something like \(r(g(\mathbf z))\) and \(r(g(T_\Psi(\mathbf c)\mathbf z))\), unless a separate classifier has been trained directly in latent space. The OCR likely garbled hats or transformed symbols, but even assuming the second \(\mathbf z\) is intended to be a transformed \(\hat{\mathbf z}\), the missing composition with the decoder leaves the method underspecified and dimensionally ambiguous. A reader trying to reproduce the objective would need to know exactly whether classification is done in input space after decoding, in latent space, or through another learned head.

comments.title: KL-to-prior regularizer does not by itself imply “largest coefficient values possible”
comments.quote: Therefore, we want to encourage the largest coefficient values possible while maintaining the identity of our initial data point. This motivates the addition of a KL-divergence regularizer that encourages the distribution _qφ_ ( **c** _|_ **z** ) to be similar to a specified Laplace prior with a fixed scale _ζ_ like in (4).
comments.explanation: The motivation is sensible: a pure consistency loss can collapse to \(\mathbf c=0\), so some pressure toward nontrivial transformations is needed. What is less clear is the stated connection between this goal and a KL regularizer to a fixed-scale Laplace prior. Matching \(q_\phi(\mathbf c\mid\mathbf z)\) to a Laplace prior with scale \(\zeta\) encourages coefficient scales near the chosen prior scale, not necessarily the largest identity-preserving values. The learned scale will depend on \(\zeta\), the KL weight, the KL direction, and the consistency penalty, so interpreting it as the maximal safe transformation extent requires additional justification. This is especially important because the following discussion uses small encoded scales to draw conclusions about local manifold structure and class-boundary proximity.

comments.title: Small local coefficient scales indicate classifier sensitivity, not necessarily true class-boundary closeness
comments.quote: Likewise, points or region that have small encoded coefficient scale weights indicate closeness to class boundaries and can undergo only small transformations without changing identity.
comments.explanation: The coefficient encoder plausibly measures how far one can move along learned operator directions before the pretrained classifier changes its prediction. The authors are careful elsewhere to phrase this in terms of classifier invariance and class prediction, which is appropriate. This final sentence goes further by interpreting small scales as closeness to class boundaries and as a limitation on identity-preserving transformations. Strictly, the learned scales reflect the pretrained classifier’s decision boundary and sensitivity along the sampled operator directions, not necessarily the true semantic class boundary or the data manifold boundary. A robust classifier, a miscalibrated classifier, or operators that leave the manifold could all change this interpretation. The claim would be safer if qualified as closeness to the classifier’s decision boundary in the learned operator directions.

comments.title: Coefficient-encoder training lacks the classifier/label specification needed to reproduce the objective
comments.quote: After completely training the MAE, we fix the autoencoder network weights and transport operator weights and train the coefficient encoder network with the objective derived in Section 3.2.
comments.explanation: The high-level sequencing is clear, and Section 3.2 has already explained that the coefficient encoder is trained using a classifier-consistency objective. However, Section 3.2 also requires a specific pretrained classifier output \(r(\cdot)\) and, in its stated setup, labeled observations \((\mathbf{z},y)\). In this experimental description it is not specified which classifier is used for this stage, whether it is the same ImageNet ResNet used for nearest-neighbor pair selection or a task-specific classifier, and what the preserved 'identity' labels are for each dataset. This is especially important for CelebA, where the paper discusses semantic attributes rather than a standard class label, and preserving a face identity is different from preserving attributes such as hair color or pale skin. The appendix may contain implementation details, but the main experimental description leaves a key methodological component ambiguous.

comments.title: Use of ImageNet features for MNIST/Fashion MNIST needs more justification relative to the stated semantic-neighbor assumption
comments.quote: We select training point pairs that are nearest neighbors in the feature space of the final, pre-logit layer of a ResNet-18 (He et al., 2015) classifier pretrained on ImageNet (Russakovsky et al., 2015).
comments.explanation: The authors' intent is understandable: pretrained classifier features are used as a perceptual/semantic metric for selecting point pairs without transformation labels. This is well motivated for natural-image datasets such as CelebA. However, earlier context frames this strategy as relying on a classifier pretrained on data with similar statistics to the target dataset. ImageNet statistics are quite different from MNIST digits and Fashion MNIST grayscale clothing images. It may still work empirically, but then the paper should clarify preprocessing and justify why ImageNet pre-logit features provide meaningful nearest neighbors for these non-natural-image datasets, or report an ablation against a domain-specific classifier or unsupervised features. Otherwise, a reader could reasonably worry that the learned operators depend on an unexamined feature-space bias rather than the proposed semantic-neighbor principle.

comments.title: Baseline interpolation/extrapolation procedure is underspecified
comments.quote: For all other methods, Eucilidean distance between features is used for both interpolation and extrapolation.
comments.explanation: The comparison goal is clear: MAE paths are generated by exponentiating inferred transport-operator coefficients, while the other methods need a corresponding path construction. But 'Euclidean distance between features' is a metric, not by itself an interpolation or extrapolation algorithm. A reproducible comparison would need to specify the exact feature representation for each baseline, the path formula, the extrapolation direction, the scale of the path parameter, and how step sizes are matched to the MAE path. This matters because linear interpolation in a \(\beta\)-VAE mean code, a standard autoencoder code, and CAE-derived features are not equivalent procedures, and extrapolation quality can be very sensitive to the chosen latent scale. The ambiguity affects an experimental comparison that is used to support a central claim about MAE extrapolation.

comments.title: CAE path construction from tangent planes is not sufficiently defined
comments.quote: The CAE-generated paths are the directions of motion on the tangent planes estimated at individual points.
comments.explanation: It is reasonable to compare MAE transport-operator directions with CAE tangent directions, since CAEs are a standard way to estimate local manifold structure. However, a tangent plane contains multiple possible directions, and the passage does not say which directions are selected, how they are normalized, how far the path is followed, or whether the tangent directions are recomputed along the path. In the CAE literature, tangent directions are typically inferred from the Jacobian structure around data points, and path generation from those local directions requires additional choices. Without those details, the visual comparison in Fig. 1 and the later interpolation/extrapolation comparison are difficult to reproduce and may depend strongly on arbitrary direction and scale choices.

comments.title: Identity-preservation metric leaves the 'correct class' and pair selection ambiguous
comments.quote: To quantify the identity preservation of each transformation, we input each generated image into a pretrained classifier and plot the prediction of the correct class.
comments.explanation: Using a pretrained classifier as a proxy for identity preservation is consistent with the paper's earlier methodology. What remains unclear is how the 'correct class' is defined for interpolated paths between two data points, and how those endpoint pairs are selected. If \(\mathbf{x}_0\) and \(\mathbf{x}_1\) always have the same class, this should be stated; if they are merely nearest neighbors in a perceptual feature space, they could in principle have different labels, in which case there is no single obvious correct class along the entire interpolation. For extrapolation, the intended label is probably the label of \(\mathbf{x}_0\), but this is also not explicit. Since Fig. 4's quantitative claim rests on this classifier-based metric, the evaluation protocol needs to define the reference label and pair-selection constraints clearly.

comments.title: Extrapolation is evaluated by classifier consistency, not true path accuracy
comments.quote: All four methods perform interpolation effectively but our trained model estimates the extrapolated paths more accurately.
comments.explanation: The surrounding text explains that generated images are passed through a pretrained classifier and that Fig. 4 tracks classification accuracy during extrapolation, so the authors likely mean that MAE extrapolations preserve class identity better. However, the phrase 'estimates the extrapolated paths more accurately' is stronger than what this metric establishes. There is generally no ground-truth extrapolated image path available, so classifier accuracy measures one aspect of identity preservation, not accuracy of the manifold path itself. A more precise claim would be that the MAE extrapolations maintain the original classifier prediction more reliably.

comments.title: Classifier-accuracy metric for interpolation paths is underspecified when endpoints may differ in class
comments.quote: Figure 4: The average accuracy of the classifier output for images at each point along the interpolation/extrapolation sequence. When the path multiplier is between 0 and 1 that indicates interpolation and path multipliers beyond 1 indicate extrapolation. (a) MNIST (b) Fashion MNIST.
comments.explanation: The intended use of this figure is clear: it is meant to quantify whether generated paths preserve identity as the path multiplier moves from interpolation to extrapolation. I first looked to the surrounding Figure 3 caption, which says the plots show the probability of the class label associated with the inputs \(\mathbf{z}_0\) and \(\mathbf{z}_1\), suggesting that the endpoints may have the same class label. However, the point-pair strategy emphasized earlier in the paper is unsupervised nearest-neighbor selection in a learned/perceptual feature space, which does not by itself guarantee same-class endpoints. For an “accuracy” along an interpolation path, it matters whether the reference label is the label of \(\mathbf{x}_0\), the label of \(\mathbf{x}_1\), only pairs where these labels agree, or the classifier’s own initial prediction. These choices can produce different curves, especially near \(t=1\) or for extrapolation. The issue is not that the metric is invalid, but that the caption/prose does not specify the target label or whether same-class pairs are enforced, so a reader cannot fully interpret or reproduce the reported identity-preservation accuracy.

comments.title: Qualitative examples alone do not establish improved identity-preserving augmentation
comments.quote: While both strategies generate some realistic variations of the data, using the encoded scale weights improves identity-preservation of the transformed output. The augmentations with the encoded scale weights are better at maintaining the identity of the sampled points.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intuition is reasonable: the coefficient encoder is trained precisely to choose local coefficient scales that preserve classifier predictions, so one would expect sampled augmentations to be safer than sampling from a single global scale. I also see that Figure 6 is presented as a qualitative illustration rather than a formal benchmark. What remains problematic is that the text states an improvement in identity preservation as a conclusion, but the passage only describes selected image examples. Since identity preservation is a central claimed benefit of the coefficient encoder, this comparison would ideally be supported by a quantitative metric, such as ground-truth label preservation, classifier-consistency rate on held-out samples, or human/attribute-based validation. In addition, the fixed-scale baseline is not described here in enough detail to judge whether it is matched fairly to the encoded-scale method. The claim may be true, but as written it is stronger than what the shown qualitative evidence alone establishes.

comments.title: Encoded scale weights are interpreted somewhat too directly as classifier invariances
comments.quote: Because the coefficient encoder uses a pretrained classifier to determine the coefficient distributions in the latent space, the resulting encoded scale weights can inform about the types of manifold transformations the classifier is invariant to. Fig. 5b shows the average coefficient scale weights for each class (rows) and each transport operator (columns) for MNIST. From this we can identify classes for which the classifier is both sensitive and robust to transformations, represented by small and large scale weights respectively.
comments.explanation: The cautious phrase “can inform” is appropriate, and the proposed visualization is a useful diagnostic: for a fixed learned operator, larger local Laplace scales plausibly indicate that larger sampled moves tend to preserve the classifier output. However, the subsequent interpretation that small/large scale weights directly represent classifier sensitivity/robustness is a bit stronger than the objective justifies. The learned scales are affected not only by classifier invariance, but also by the normalization and magnitude of each transport operator, the KL regularizer toward the fixed Laplace prior, the decoder/latent geometry, the sampling procedure used during coefficient-encoder training, and classifier calibration/confidence. In particular, coefficient scales are most interpretable comparatively within the same operator; comparing scale magnitudes across different operators can be misleading unless operator norms or transformation effects are normalized. The qualitative examples help, but the text should retain these caveats when treating the heatmap as evidence of classifier invariance.
